title: Kenya's SHA suspends M.P. Shah Hospital over billing complaints, pending investigation | HealthCare Middle East & Africa - No.1 Health & Pharma Manufacturing Industry Publication
summary: The move affects services offered under the Primary Healthcare Fund (PHCF), Social Health Insurance Fund (SHIF), Emergency, Chronic and Critical Illness Fund (ECCIF), and the Public Officers Medic ...
thbAction: Map the news to THB's provider CRM for referral growth, patient lifecycle engagement, and multi-facility relationship management across hospital and provider networks.
contentHtml:
KENYA—M.P. Shah Hospital has been temporarily suspended from providing services to beneficiaries of the Social Health Authority (SHA) and the Social Health Insurance Fund (SHIF) following complaints related to billing practices.

The suspension, which took effect on June 23, 2026, will remain in place while SHA conducts investigations into the allegations.

The move affects services offered under the Primary Healthcare Fund (PHCF), Social Health Insurance Fund (SHIF), Emergency, Chronic and Critical Illness Fund (ECCIF), and the Public Officers Medical Scheme Fund (POMSF).

In a notice issued to beneficiaries and healthcare stakeholders, SHA Chief Executive Officer Dr. Mercy Mwangangi stated that the authority had removed the hospital from its panel of contracted healthcare providers pending the outcome of the investigations.

The authority emphasized that the action is intended to safeguard public healthcare resources and ensure accountability within the healthcare system.

Hospital responds and launches internal review

M.P. Shah Hospital acknowledged receiving the suspension notice and said it would fully cooperate with the ongoing investigations.

In a statement, the hospital said it had already initiated an internal review of its billing processes. Management also stressed that patients should not bear costs that are meant to be covered under their SHIF benefits.

The hospital noted that it takes the concerns seriously and is committed to identifying and correcting any shortcomings that may have affected patients.

It added that every patient deserves access to the healthcare services they are entitled to without facing unnecessary financial burdens.

To minimize disruptions, M.P. Shah Hospital said its teams are assisting affected patients and coordinating safe transfers to other SHA-contracted facilities where necessary.

Guidance for patients during the suspension

SHA has assured beneficiaries admitted before the suspension date that they will continue to receive treatment at M.P. Shah Hospital until the completion of their current episode of care and discharge.

However, patients seeking new admissions, follow-up treatment, outpatient services, or other healthcare services during the suspension period have been advised to seek care at alternative facilities contracted by SHA.

 Beneficiaries requiring assistance with referrals or continuity of care can access support through SHA’s established communication channels. 

Suspension comes amid wider oversight reforms

The suspension comes as the government intensifies efforts to strengthen oversight of Kenya’s healthcare financing system following the transition from the National Health Insurance Fund (NHIF) to SHA and SHIF.

In recent months, health authorities have increased scrutiny of healthcare providers over suspected fraud, overbilling, and misuse of public health insurance funds.

SHA has also introduced artificial intelligence-based monitoring systems designed to identify suspicious claims, unnecessary procedures, and irregular billing patterns.

According to reports, the technology has already flagged billions of shillings in questionable claims for further review.

The development follows earlier enforcement actions against other healthcare facilities accused of fraudulent claims practices as the authority seeks to improve transparency and accountability across the sector.

Hospital expands services despite regulatory challenge

The suspension comes just days after M.P. Shah Hospital unveiled The Hub Medical Centre in Karen, Nairobi, a new outpatient facility designed to improve access to specialised healthcare services for residents of Karen and surrounding areas.

The center is expected to provide a range of medical services closer to communities while supporting the hospital’s broader expansion strategy.
